Quote coronets and silks verbatim. Don't paraphrase pace dockets — give a faithful one-liner for each. Be terse.

15	## Design decisions captured

17	### GAR categorical layout
18	
19	Three top-level namespaces under the cloud prefix:

21	```

23	<prefix>reliquaries/<date>/<tool>
24	<prefix>enshrines/<base>
25	```
26	
27	Each immediate child of `<prefix>hallmarks` IS a hallmark; the six ark types become plain basename siblings. No more vessel-in-path or suffix-on-tag string grammar.
28	
29	### Vessel identity
30	
31	No longer lives in the GAR path. Resolved on demand from the `vouch` or `about` metadata of the hallmark in question. `ls <prefix>hallmarks/` no longer surfaces vessel at a glance; tally and plumb already read metadata for health, and extend the pattern where operation prose needs the vessel name.
32	
33	### Attest vs vouch
34	
35	Both persist as peer basenames under `<hallmark>/`. Attestations advertise during build for SLSA provenance; vouches check them at consumption. Load-bearing split — retained by design.
36	
37	### Pouch
38	
39	A `FROM scratch` OCI image carrying build context (Dockerfile + supporting files) to Cloud Build's first step. Present for conjure/bind, absent for graft (graft is local push — no GCB involved). Now lives at `<prefix>hallmarks/<hallmark>/pouch` in the new layout.
40

42	
43	`RBGC_ARK_SUFFIX_*` constants persist but rename to `RBGC_ARK_BASENAME_*`; their values drop the leading hyphen. Role shifts from "suffix to hyphen-concat" to "literal basename of a sibling file." The concat grammar disappears entirely from ark path construction.

46	
47	`RBGC_VOUCHES_PACKAGE` existed to enable hallmark-only vouch lookup without knowing the vessel. The new layout delivers the same property naturally — every hallmark has its own `vouch` basename under `<prefix>hallmarks/<hallmark>/`. The aggregator becomes redundant and is deleted.

506	(Note: also brace the other three printf args in this block while we're here — full BCG sweep on the 4-line block.)
507	
508	`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh:264` — current block (lines ~260-267):
509	
510	```bash

200	  rbmu_users/
201	    <BURH-style profiles>
202	  rbmv_vessels/
203	    common-sentry-context/
204	    rbev-bottle-ifrit/              <- kit-shipped, keeps rbev- provenance marker
205	    rbev-bottle-plantuml/ ...
206	    <user vessels without rbev- prefix>
207	Tools/

210	    rbob_bottle.sh

212	  buk/ ...
213	tt/
214	  z-launcher.sh                     <- trampoline, hardcodes ../rbmm_moorings/rbml_launchers/
215	  rbw-*.sh ...                      <- tabtargets route through z-launcher
216	```
217	
218	### Trampoline design
219	
220	Every tabtarget currently hardcodes `.buk/launcher_*_workbench.sh` — the single most pervasive path reference in the system. Introducing `tt/z-launcher.sh` as a stable indirection means future moorings-directory renames touch exactly one file. Tabtargets call the trampoline with a workbench identifier; the trampoline resolves `../rbmm_moorings/rbml_launchers/launcher.{id}.sh` (or equivalent).
221	
222	The `rbmm_moorings/` path inside `z-launcher.sh` is hardcoded. Customers adjust it at project start if they choose to rename the moorings directory. That's a deliberate single-point-of-customization.
223	
224	### Rejected / decided alternatives
225	
226	- **Kit subdirectories inside moorings** (`rbmm_moorings/rbk/`, `rbmm_moorings/buk/`) — rejected. File prefixes (`burc.`, `rbrr.`, `rbrn.`) already encode kit; subdirs would be redundant nesting, and the kit boundary is a producer concern not a consumer one.
227	- **Separate `regime/` and `vessels/` top-level dirs** — rejected. Single `rbmm_moorings/` umbrella is cleaner. Vessels are consumer-configured (even if rarely edited), belong with the rest of the configuration surface.
228	- **`rbmb_` bootstrap-constants module** — rejected. Original `rbbc_constants.sh` existed to single-source the `.rbk` literal across many files. With `rbmm_moorings/` as the standardized, z-launcher-hardcoded name, no cross-file indirection is needed.
229	- **Bridge period (both old and new paths work transiently)** — rejected. Hard cutover is cleaner.
230	- **Extend moorings to JJK/CMK/VVK** — rejected. `rbmm_moorings/` is RBK+BUK only. JJK uses `.claude/jjm/` for agent-side state (different kettle). Other kits keep their own conventions.
231	- **Visible `config/` or `regime/`** — rejected. `config/` throws away the project's distinctive vocabulary; `regime/` is too narrow (regimes are formal configurations; moorings also contains bootstrap glue and vessels).
232	
233	### Prefix coexistence note
234	
235	Inside `rbmm_moorings/`, regime files retain their original family prefixes (`burc_`, `rbrr_`, `rbrn_`, `rbrp_`). The `rbm*_` family is the moorings-infrastructure prefix — directories and any new moorings-level code. This is a feature, not a conflict: regime file prefixes tell you which kit's regime you're reading; `rbm*_` tells you it's moorings infrastructure.
236	
237	## Migration scope (for the follow-up rename heat)
238	
239	The actual filesystem rename is out of scope for this heat (₣A_). It is intended as a separate follow-up heat. The migration scope below is captured here so the follow-up heat has a starting inventory.

The single tabtarget `tt/z-launcher.sh` resolves the moorings location and dispatches to the named launcher; all other tabtargets invoke it with a workbench identifier. This makes the moorings location a single-point-of-customization at the tabtarget layer, parallel to BURC providing it at the CLI layer.

Add `ZRBFC_ENDPOINT_MANIFESTS="manifests"`, `ZRBFC_ENDPOINT_BLOBS="blobs"`, `ZRBFC_ENDPOINT_TAGS_LIST="tags/list"`, `ZRBFC_API_VERSION="v2"` at RBFC tinder-constant position (post-`SOURCED`, pre-kindle). 687	### bcg-shellcheck-cross-module-discipline (₢A_AAY) [rough]
688	
689	## Character
690	
691	Simple-pattern discipline. BCG exists because nuanced bash craftsmanship gets forgotten across chat boundaries and corrupts. The shape of the answer here should be a uniform, easily-verifiable rule baked into BCG — not a per-script judgment call. Bias toward the simplest mechanical pattern. Only consider a more-nuanced shape if there's a concrete reason against the default.
692	
693	## Docket
694	
695	Cross-module readonly state (Z*_PREFIX, RB*_* constants) is shared across BCG-compliant modules via the kindle/sentinel chain. Shellcheck cannot follow runtime sourcing and fires SC2153 ("possible misspelling") on every cross-module reference. ₢A_AAN's F6b addresses this per-file in two scripts (`rbgp_Payor.sh`, `rbgg_Governor.sh`). This pace decides whether to promote the fix to a BCG-level pattern.
696	
697	### Default answer
698	
699	A single mandated line in BCG's prologue spec for kindled modules:
700	
701	    # shellcheck disable=SC2153  # cross-module Z*_/RB*_ state via kindle chain
702	
703	Placed below the copyright header, above `set -euo pipefail`. Uniform across all BCG modules. Verifiable by `grep -L 'shellcheck disable=SC2153' Tools/**/*.sh` (find files missing the directive).
704	
705	### Decision criterion
706	
707	Adopt the default unless one of these holds:
708	
709	- A meaningful fraction of BCG modules do NOT participate in cross-module state sharing (the directive becomes dead weight in leaf modules — silent typo-detection loss for no benefit).
710	- A survey finds existing typo bugs that SC2153 *would* have caught (suggests the false-positive rate is acceptable and the warning carries real value).
711	
712	If neither holds, apply the default. If either holds, report the finding and slate a follow-up — do not invent a middle ground.
713	
714	### Work items
715	
716	1. **Survey** — count BCG-compliant modules in the tree; spot-check ~10 for whether they reference cross-module readonly state. Estimate participation rate.
717	2. **Decide** using the criterion above. Record the outcome in one line.
718	3. **If applying:** amend BCG (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`), sweep the directive across all BCG modules, remove pre-existing per-file SC2153 disables (including those landed by AAN F6b). Single notch.
719	4. **If not applying:** report why; slate a follow-up if a different shape is warranted.
720	5. **Verify** — shellcheck delta across the tree matches expectation; a deliberate scratch-script typo confirms `set -u` still catches what SC2153 used to catch.
721	
722	## Why simple
723	
724	The architectural alternatives (`.shellcheckrc` repo config, `# shellcheck source=...` per-reference directives) preserve more typo detection but cost more cognitive load and survive chat boundaries less well. BCG's track record favors verifiable uniformity over engineering nuance. Stay there unless evidence forces otherwise.
725	
726	## Out of scope
727	
728	- SC2154 disable (different shellcheck class, different tradeoff).
729	- `.shellcheckrc` repo-level config.
730	- `# shellcheck source=...` directives.
731	- Bringing non-BCG scripts into BCG compliance.
732	
733	## References
734	
735	- ₢A_AAN F6b — becomes redundant if the default is applied.
736	- BCG: `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`.
737	- CLAUDE.md "Load-Bearing Complexity" — uniformity is what's load-bearing here, not technical sophistication.

741	## Character
742	Operational — live GCP infrastructure change, requires payor credentials. The forcing function for the entire heat: AAA–AAD all land before this; this regen validates the new prefix wiring and GAR layout end-to-end against fresh infrastructure.
743

746	Before running, set non-empty prefixes in `.rbk/rbrr.env` (so the regen exercises non-empty paths, not the empty-prefix shortcut):
747	
748	```
749	RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX="rbm-"
750	RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX="dev-"
751	```
752	
753	Distinct values for cloud and runtime are deliberate — proves the variables are wired distinctly, not aliased through any common derivation. `rbm-` matches the project family prefix; `dev-` signals local-machine scope. Both are 4 chars (well under the 11-char ceiling) and pass the regex.

967	
968	### Open design question — resolve at pace start
969	
970	`RBRV_RELIQUARY=<stamp>` tells the precheck WHICH reliquary, but not WHICH tools from it the vessel consumes. Three options, pick one:
971	
972	1. **Vessel-regime extension**: add `RBRV_RELIQUARY_TOOLS` (or similar) declaring the tool list. Most explicit; requires regime schema work and per-vessel docket updates.
973	2. **Full-subtree presence check**: precheck enumerates every image under `<prefix>reliquaries/<stamp>/` and verifies all are present. Catches piecemeal jettison on the whole stamp; doesn't distinguish tools the vessel actually needs from tools it doesn't. Conservative — flags integrity damage even on tools the vessel ignores.
974	3. **Cloudbuild-substitution parse**: derive tool list from the rendered cloudbuild.json's `_RBG?_*` tool-image substitutions before submission. Brittle (parser tracks producer-side grammar) but precise.
975	
976	Recommend option 2 for v1 simplicity (any tool missing means the stamp's integrity is questionable; conservative failure is acceptable). Reconsider if false-positive rate is high in practice.
977	
978	### Operation
979	
980	Before Cloud Build submission in conjure and bind ordain modes:
981	
982	1. Resolve the vessel's `RBRV_IMAGE_ORIGIN` against `<prefix>enshrines/<base>` — confirm the enshrined base image exists at the expected GAR path.
983	2. If the vessel is reliquary-yoked (`RBRV_RELIQUARY` set), apply the chosen tool-list resolution (above) and confirm each expected image is present.
984	3. On any miss, exit non-zero before any Cloud Build call. Error message format (nuclear advice only):
985	
986	```
987	PRECHECK: GAR image not found at <prefix>enshrines/<base>
988	  Required by <vessel>'s RBRV_IMAGE_ORIGIN.
989	  Recover by re-enshrining: tt/rbw-dE.DirectorEnshrinesVessel.sh
990	```
991	
992	```
993	PRECHECK: GAR image not found at <prefix>reliquaries/<stamp>/<tool>
994	  Required by <vessel>'s RBRV_RELIQUARY=<stamp>.
995	  Recover by re-inscribing: tt/rbw-dI.DirectorInscribesReliquary.sh
996	```
997	
998	No surgical recovery hints. If a ref is broken, the operator re-creates the whole reliquary or re-enshrines the base — the cosmology accepts that piecemeal jettison is allowed but unrecoverable surgically.

1001	
1002	`rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` (or its caller in `rbfd_cli.sh`). Add a function `zrbfd_precheck_refs <vessel-path> <mode>` invoked at the start of conjure and bind paths. Caller passes the mode; function does not redetect mode from regime. Function reads vessel's RBRV regime, performs HEAD requests against the resolved GAR paths, accumulates misses, prints the structured error message(s), exits non-zero on any miss.

1007	
1008	- **Conjure** — precheck: enshrined base required; reliquary refs if yoked.
1009	- **Bind** — precheck: enshrined base required; reliquary refs if yoked.
1010	- **Graft** — skip: graft is local-image-push, no GCB-side ref consumption.
1011	- **Enshrine** (`rbw-dE`) — skip: input is upstream image (Docker Hub / similar), not GAR.
1012	- **Inscribe** (`rbw-dI`) — skip: input is upstream tool images, not GAR.
1013	- **Yoke** (`rbw-dY`) — out of scope for this pace. Yoke-time stamp existence check is a sensible follow-up; surface as paddock note if the gap matters in practice.

1016	
1017	Per-call HEAD requests scale with reliquary size. For a vessel yoked to a stamp with 20 tool images, precheck adds ~21 HEAD requests (1 enshrine + 20 reliquary) before any Cloud Build call. Expected sub-second total against GAR. Acceptable overhead given the failure-mode-cost asymmetry (failed Cloud Build wastes minutes-to-hours of build time).

1021	- Negative: jettison the enshrined base of a test vessel, attempt conjure → precheck fails with the structured message; no Cloud Build submission occurs (verify by absence of build in Cloud Build console).
1022	- Negative: jettison one tool image from a reliquary, attempt bind on a vessel yoked to that reliquary → precheck fails with structured message naming the missing tool.

## Candidate placements for new "Cloud Build machineType benchmark" pace

**Slot A — between ₢A_AAP and ₢A_AAN (after live burn-in, before AAN's mechanical sweep)**: AAP already drives multiple full ordain cycles against live Cloud Build; a benchmark pace immediately after fits the "investigative posture" already in flight and gives baseline timing data while infrastructure context is fresh. Not the cleanest, though — AAN/AAY are pure code hygiene that don't depend on the timing.

**Slot B — between ₢A_AAW and ₢A_AAX (during the imageops sequence)**: AAW adds precheck HEAD requests before CB submission and explicitly worries about the "minutes-to-hours of build time" failure-mode cost. A benchmark pace here gives concrete numbers backing AAW's cost reasoning, and AAX's new lifecycle fixtures could optionally key off the chosen machineType. Most thematically adjacent — but late in the heat.

**Slot C — between ₢A_AAF and ₢A_AAG (after complete test suite, before onboarding walkthrough)**: AAF has just rebuilt every kit-shipped vessel against a fresh depot — the heat's most concentrated burst of real Cloud Build runs. Benchmarking here piggybacks on builds that already need to happen, minimizing extra Cloud Build minutes spent. Strong cost-efficiency argument.

## Character/posture note — does this fit ₣A_ at all?

**It probably does not.** The paddock's three interwoven threads are all about *correctness* of prefix wiring and GAR layout under hard-cutover discipline, with AAE as a forcing function. Nothing in the paddock or the existing pace dockets touches build-performance, Cloud Build resource sizing, or cost optimization — the heat's posture is "ship the migration cleanly," not "tune what's there." The heat's overall character (load-bearing migration with hard-cutover discipline) clashes with a curiosity-driven benchmark.

A standalone heat (e.g., `rbk-cloudbuild-machinetype-benchmark` or a broader `rbk-build-perf-survey` heat) would honor JJ's "bounded initiative with coherent goals" framing better. The benchmark is itch-shaped — capture as an itch in `jji_itch.md`, or nominate a dedicated heat — rather than retrofit it into ₣A_'s migration arc.

If it must land in ₣A_, Slot C is the most defensible (piggyback on builds AAF must run anyway). Otherwise: nominate separately.
